title: Frame loom soumak weave
subtitle: Building a diagonal twill-effect surface on the frame loom
excerpt: Soumak is a wrapped-weft technique that produces a diagonal, braided surface texture on the frame loom warp. Unlike standard tabby tapestry, the soumak weft wraps around two warp threads before stepping back one. This entry covers the three standard soumak directions and how to combine them for pattern.
sourceNotes: Soumak weave documented in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in Caucasian and Middle Eastern flatweave scholarship held by the V&A Museum. The technique is among the oldest recorded weft-wrapping methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The vertical threads stretched on the frame before weaving. In soumak, the warp threads are the posts around which the weft wraps.
The horizontal yarn that travels across the warp. In soumak, the weft wraps rather than interlaces, producing a raised, angled surface structure.
A weft-wrapping technique in which the weft passes forward over two warp threads, then back under one, producing a diagonal chain of loops on the surface. Named after a town in the Caucasus region.
Plain weave, used as a locking row between soumak rows to hold the structure stable. Without tabby between each soumak pass, the wrapped weft can slide on the warp.

threads, then back under one, moving forward by one thread net per wrap. Each row of

items.name: Frame loom with a 5 ends-per-inch warp
Tapestry weft yarn in one or more colours
Locking tabby yarn (can match or contrast)

content.text: Working left to right: bring the weft yarn up between threads 1 and 2. Pass it over threads 2 and 3 from left to right. Bring it back between threads 2 and 3. The weft has now wrapped around thread 2 and is ready to advance. Move to the right and repeat: over threads 3 and 4, back between 3 and 4. Continue across the full row. Each wrap produces one angled loop on the surface, slanting from lower-left to upper-right.

The same action worked from right to left, or worked right to left with the wrap sequence reversed, produces loops slanting in the opposite direction. A chevron can be built by working forward soumak from the selvedge to the centre, then reversing to backward soumak from the centre to the other selvedge. When the two directions meet at the centre, the loops form a V shape.

After every soumak row, weave one row of plain tabby before beginning the next soumak pass. The tabby row locks the soumak wraps and prevents the whole surface from shifting upward. Use a finer yarn in a matching colour for the tabby rows so they are not visible on the face of the piece.

items.problem: The soumak rows slide upward and bunch together
items.cause: No tabby locking row between soumak passes
items.fix: Add a plain tabby row between every soumak row without exception. The tabby row is not visible but is structurally essential.

items.problem: The soumak loops look uneven in length
items.cause: Inconsistent tension on the weft wrap
items.fix: Hold the weft at a consistent angle and pull each wrap to the same tension before moving to the next pair of threads. Work slowly until the rhythm is established.
